name: CodePipeline
description: Fully managed continuous delivery service for automated release pipelines
key_facts: Stages contain action groups that run sequentially or in parallel
Supports source, build, test, deploy, approval, and invoke action types
Manual approval actions for gated deployments
Pipeline execution history and state transitions tracked automatically
Cross-account and cross-region pipeline actions supported
common_misconceptions: CodePipeline performs builds itself (it orchestrates; CodeBuild performs the builds)
Pipeline stages must be sequential (action groups within a stage can run in parallel)

name: CodeBuild
description: Fully managed build service that compiles source code, runs tests, and produces artifacts
key_facts: buildspec.yml defines build phases: install, pre_build, build, post_build
Supports custom Docker images for build environments
Build artifacts stored in S3; logs in CloudWatch Logs
Local caching (source, Docker layer, custom) reduces build times
VPC support enables access to private resources during builds
common_misconceptions: CodeBuild requires managing build servers (it is fully serverless)
Build environment is persistent between builds (each build runs in a fresh container)

name: CodeDeploy Deployment Strategies
description: Blue/green, canary, and rolling deployments for EC2, Lambda, and ECS
key_facts: EC2/On-premises: in-place or blue/green deployment types
Lambda: canary, linear, and all-at-once traffic shifting
ECS: canary, linear, and all-at-once with replacement task sets
AppSpec file defines lifecycle hooks and deployment instructions
Automatic rollback on deployment failure or CloudWatch alarm trigger
common_misconceptions: Blue/green for EC2 reuses existing instances (it provisions entirely new instances)
Canary and linear deployments are only for Lambda (ECS also supports them)

name: Canary and Blue/Green Deployments
description: Progressive deployment patterns with traffic shifting and automated rollback
key_facts: Canary: shifts small percentage first (e.g., 10%), then remaining after validation
Linear: shifts traffic in equal increments at regular intervals
Blue/green: runs two identical environments, switches all traffic at once
CloudWatch alarms can trigger automatic rollback during traffic shifting
ALB weighted target groups enable fine-grained traffic distribution
common_misconceptions: Canary always means 5% traffic (percentage and interval are configurable)
Blue/green deployment doubles infrastructure cost permanently (the old environment is terminated after validation)

name: CloudFormation Advanced Features
description: Nested stacks, StackSets, drift detection, and custom resources
key_facts: Nested stacks enable component reuse and modular templates
StackSets deploy stacks across multiple accounts and regions simultaneously
Drift detection identifies resources modified outside CloudFormation
Custom resources invoke Lambda for unsupported resource types
cfn-signal and CreationPolicy coordinate with instance bootstrapping
common_misconceptions: StackSets update all instances simultaneously (they update sequentially by default with configurable concurrency)
Drift detection runs automatically (it must be manually initiated or scheduled)

name: AWS CDK
description: Define cloud infrastructure using familiar programming languages
key_facts: Supports TypeScript, Python, Java, C#, and Go
Constructs: L1 (CFN resources), L2 (opinionated defaults), L3 (patterns)
cdk synth generates CloudFormation templates from code
cdk diff shows infrastructure changes before deployment
Assets (Docker images, Lambda code) are automatically uploaded to S3/ECR
common_misconceptions: CDK replaces CloudFormation (CDK generates CloudFormation templates under the hood)
CDK constructs are only for simple resources (L3 constructs define complex multi-resource patterns)

name: Service Catalog
description: Self-service portal of approved IT products for governance at scale
key_facts: Products are defined as CloudFormation templates with versioning
Portfolios group products and control access via IAM
Launch constraints define the IAM role used when provisioning products
Shared portfolios enable cross-account product distribution
TagOptions enforce tagging standards on provisioned resources
common_misconceptions: End users need CloudFormation knowledge to use Service Catalog (they interact through a simplified portal)
Service Catalog products can only be CloudFormation (it also supports Terraform via external engine)

name: Multi-Region DR Strategies
description: Disaster recovery architectures with defined RTO and RPO targets
key_facts: Backup and restore: lowest cost, highest RTO/RPO (hours)
Pilot light: minimal core infrastructure running in DR region
Warm standby: scaled-down but fully functional copy in DR region
Multi-site active/active: full production in multiple regions, lowest RTO/RPO
RTO: time to recover; RPO: acceptable data loss window
common_misconceptions: Pilot light and warm standby are the same (pilot light runs minimal infrastructure; warm standby is a scaled-down full copy)
Active/active eliminates all data loss (network partitions can still cause brief inconsistency)

name: Chaos Engineering and Fault Injection
description: Testing system resilience through controlled fault injection experiments
key_facts: AWS Fault Injection Service (FIS) runs controlled experiments
Experiment templates define actions, targets, and stop conditions
Supports EC2 instance termination, CPU/memory stress, network disruption
Stop conditions halt experiments if safety thresholds are breached
Integrates with CloudWatch alarms for automatic experiment termination
common_misconceptions: Chaos engineering means randomly breaking things in production (experiments are controlled with safety guardrails)
FIS only works with EC2 (it supports ECS, EKS, RDS, and more)

name: CloudWatch Advanced Monitoring
description: CloudWatch Logs Insights, Container Insights, and custom metrics for deep observability
key_facts: Logs Insights supports SQL-like queries across multiple log groups
Container Insights collects ECS and EKS metrics at cluster, service, and task level
Embedded Metric Format (EMF) publishes custom metrics from structured log events
Contributor Insights identifies top contributors (e.g., top IP addresses, top URLs)
Anomaly detection uses ML to create metric baselines and alert on deviations
common_misconceptions: Container Insights is enabled by default (it must be explicitly enabled per cluster)
CloudWatch Logs Insights queries run in real-time (they query historical log data)

name: X-Ray Distributed Tracing
description: End-to-end tracing for analyzing and debugging distributed applications
key_facts: Traces follow requests across services (API Gateway, Lambda, ECS, EC2)
Segments represent work done by a service; subsegments add detail
Service map visualizes application architecture and latency
Sampling rules control the percentage of requests traced to manage costs
Annotations enable trace filtering; metadata adds non-indexed detail
common_misconceptions: X-Ray traces every request by default (default sampling traces the first request per second plus 5% of additional requests)
X-Ray replaces CloudWatch (X-Ray is for tracing; CloudWatch is for metrics and logs)

name: EventBridge Auto-Remediation
description: Event-driven automated incident response and remediation patterns
key_facts: EventBridge rules trigger Lambda functions for automated remediation
AWS Health events can trigger automated responses to service disruptions
Config rule compliance changes can trigger remediation workflows
SNS fan-out pattern enables parallel notification and remediation
Step Functions orchestrate multi-step remediation runbooks
common_misconceptions: EventBridge can only trigger Lambda (it supports 20+ target types including Step Functions, SQS, SNS, ECS tasks)
Automated remediation replaces incident response processes (it handles known issues; novel incidents still need human judgment)

name: SSM Automation Runbooks
description: Predefined and custom operational runbooks for automated incident resolution
key_facts: Automation documents define multi-step operational procedures
AWS provides 300+ pre-built runbooks for common operational tasks
Supports approval workflows for change management
Rate control limits concurrent execution across a fleet
Can be invoked by EventBridge, Config remediation, or manually
common_misconceptions: SSM Automation and Run Command are the same (Automation runs multi-step workflows; Run Command executes single commands)
Custom runbooks require coding in a programming language (they use YAML or JSON document format)

name: Secrets Manager Rotation
description: Automated secret rotation for database credentials and API keys
key_facts: Automatic rotation uses Lambda functions on a configurable schedule
Built-in rotation templates for RDS, Redshift, and DocumentDB
Multi-user rotation strategy for zero-downtime credential updates
Secrets versioned with staging labels (AWSCURRENT, AWSPENDING, AWSPREVIOUS)
Resource-based policies control cross-account secret access
common_misconceptions: Secrets Manager and Parameter Store are interchangeable (Secrets Manager has built-in rotation; Parameter Store does not)
Rotation happens instantly (the rotation Lambda goes through createSecret, setSecret, testSecret, finishSecret steps)

name: GuardDuty and Security Automation
description: Automated threat detection and response using GuardDuty with Config conformance packs
key_facts: GuardDuty analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs for threats
Findings published to EventBridge for automated response workflows
Config conformance packs bundle rules for compliance frameworks (PCI-DSS, HIPAA)
Suppression rules filter expected GuardDuty findings to reduce noise
Delegated administrator model for multi-account GuardDuty management
common_misconceptions: GuardDuty prevents attacks (it detects threats; prevention requires additional remediation)
Config conformance packs enforce compliance (they detect non-compliance; SCPs enforce)
